speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Irene Gregory, December 5th, 1996. This is cassette number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my husband, Boris Gregory, sitting next to me, my daughter, Claudia Gregory Werlin, and my son, Michael Gregory. And the others are absent. They live in Southern California. That's Ronald Gregory and Leonard Gregory. Unfortunately, they couldn't be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so happy that all of us are, almost, together here and very, very happy to see that everybody is in good health.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my paternal grandmother, Jennie Vorreufer. She was born last century. I'm not sure when. I have it on paper somewhere. She was married to Isidore. And together, they shared the name Baruch. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is Isidore Baruch, Jennie Vorreufer's husband, who was the Director of the Elsbach Shirt Company in Herford, Westphalia. And they were married. And she died much earlier than he did. He died in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said something about their graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you want to hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, great, OK. Go ahead. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: They are buried in the Jewish cemetery in Herford. And the graves are taken care of by the city of Herford. And they are in very good shape. I went and looked. A woman who worked in their household personally saw to it that they would have fresh flowers. But she was in her 90s and died recently, so I'm hoping that the city will keep it up. This is a photograph of Marie Adrienne Teblee Baruch, my mother. And that is my brother, I think approximately three years old, four years old. The photograph must have been taken just before their move to Berlin from Herford. So that puts it at 1926, '27. This small photograph was given to my family by friends, since we didn't have any of this when we came. This shows my mother and-- my mother, Marie Adrienne Baruch, and myself, Irene, at that time, Ilse-- I may have been 10 years old, possibly, or younger-- on our balcony in Berlin, Number 11, Nymphenburger Strasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's somebody else. How about that? My brother-- four years younger than I. His name is John now. At that time, it was Hans. I forgot about him. [INAUDIBLE] This is my father, Bernard Baruch, on the same balcony. On his left is John Baruch. On his right is myself, Irene Gregory now, on the same balcony in Berlin. This is a picture myself at age 14, 15, taken in a school for young ladies outside of Munich, in Oberstdorf. This was the year 1938, summertime. And I may have been 14, 15 years old. This is an ID card with the insignia J and Z, for Juden and Zigeuner. This was, instead of a passport, to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gypsy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an ID card, because that's all a Jew was supposed to have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why don't we open it? This is the inside of that ID card, with my father's picture and his full name-- Bernard Israel Baruch-- issued because of-- because Jews were not given passports. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's just an ID card. And apparently, it was valid until 1944, which is neither here nor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what's-- what's the significance of Israel on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all Jews-- women were given the middle name Sarah, and men, Israel, to be sure that nobody could mistake them for anything but Jews. This piece of paper was issued by the Commandant of the camp-- Camp Gurs in the Pyrenees-- where my mother and I were interned. This is my mother's name, Baruch Marie Adrienne. She-- she and I both were released to my father, who was in uniform, therefore not to be questioned. And the date is on there and all other incidental remarks-- Certificate of Liberation. This piece of paper is another ID. It's called sauf-conduit in French and was issued by the French authorities upon our departure from France. Destination, it has Unis, which means United States. And what is noticeable is that Bernard Baruch shed the middle name of Israel. He was on his way to the New World. This is another ID. This one is for my mother-- Marie Adrienne Baruch, nee Teblee, by the French authorities. Notice, again, that the word Sarah is no longer there. It seems that this picture is not-- wasn't current-- it looks younger than she must have been. However, they were on their way to the New World. My parents, Bernard Baruch and Marie Adrienne Baruch, Rockaway Beach, New York, after their arrival in New York, taking a stroll. This, again, is Rockaway Beach. This must be 1942. It's Marie Adrienne Baruch and her two children. I'm in the middle, Irene, now Gregory. And John Baruch on the outside. I made that outfit. Bernard Baruch in New York in the beginnings, probably 1942, '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hopefully working by then and in good health. My father's obituary, probably from the which was a German paper for German refugees to read. He died February 16th, 1958, at age 62. And it refers to his wife, his two children, and his grandchildren. Wait, wait, wait. My claim to fame-- a cookbook written by me when I was in a ladies' finishing school in 1938. It's totally handwritten, and the recipes are rather useless at this time. This is rather self-explanatory. It's a pretty full book, filled with unhealthy recipes, but very, very delicious, I am sure-- lots of butter and eggs. And that was one of the projects at that school. The reason we thought we'd show this is because this is one of the many paintings my parents owned. And it tells, in her handwriting, that the Nazis stole it from her in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably-- I don't know why-- 1941. But in essence, the artist, Alexander [? Kerster, ?] is advising her how to pack this painting for transport. It had a glass over it, and she was concerned that it would break. It was a correspondence. In fact, there were many, many paintings that are missing. This is one of the many letters I have from my father to my mother while she was interned. She and I were interned in the camp Gurs, in the Pyrenees. It's a very tender card that's in French. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What, in fact, this says is that he was thrilled to find out where his women were, and that he will try to keep in touch and hopefully see us soon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was addressed to the camp. This is the address on the other side of the card. Ilot G was where we were situated in this camp, which was vast. And that's how we were identified. And he wrote it-- he was, by then, a French soldier and wrote it from-- on the road somewhere. This is a military ID from the First World War, belonging to my father, who went to war with his own horse and groom-- German cavalry-- and bears all those stamps and insignia. The paper on the right shows that we were passengers on the ship called [? Guinea, ?] in Portuguese registry. It was a coastal steamer equipped for 75 and took on 500 passengers from Casablanca to New York. It was called the [? Guinea. ?] The other papers show that we were in perfectly good health, because they were medical examinations. And they bear the proper stamps and were issued on December 26th, 1941. Interestingly enough, this is a medical certification issued by Doctor Ernst Israel Hirschfeld Berlin-Schoneberg, who was only allowed to treat Jews. This is the month of February-- 15th of February-- 1939 and certifies that Ilse Baruch, myself, was mentally and physically not defective in any way. I didn't have tuberculosis, either and no other contagious disease. It's in English. It's amazing. This certifies that my father was politically clean. There was nothing on his record that might prevent his emigrating from Berlin. And the date on this is February 20th, 1939, with all the proper stamps and how much it cost to issue this. This looks like one mark. This is the last piece of paper that my father received upon his leaving Marseilles. It's instead of a passport. That's all the ID we had on us at that time. Issued in Marseilles, December of '41. This is an account of the things we need-- needed to post before we left Berlin. It tells of Dr. Bernard Israel Baruch, who resided in Nymphenburger Strasse 11, Berlin-Schoneberg. It's part, of-- I think-- of what we were allowed to take. And it's a detailed account of some of the things. This is a notice my father, Bernard Baruch, made-- all the directions that we were trying to get away. And it shows a train schedule, I believe-- which days. And you can see that he scheduled it from Cannes to Toulouse, trying to go through Madrid, through Lisbon. None of this happened, actually, because we were forced to leave through Marseilles, which is second from the top and never made it through Spain and Lisbon, which is all right. This is my parents' whole life, in this briefcase. We took it wherever we went, never left it anywhere. It went through immigration with my parents and contained a huge amount of correspondence and all their passports or sauf-conduits, and tells the tale.
